coredata.dc:title: Effect of graphene quantum dots addition on photoelectric performances of CdSSe
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, Yuanyuan
Jiang, Zicong
Ouyang, Zhaohui
Lei, Yun
Ouyang, Zhong
Hu, Jiaxin
Du, Peng
Wu, Yuncui
coredata.dc:description: CdSSe/graphene quantum dots (CdSSe/GQDs) composites were prepared by a hydrothermal method in the way of electrostatic interaction. The structure, morphology and material performance were investigated by various physical and chemical analyses. According to the results of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, the GQDs were compounded into composites. Moreover, the composites contained C–O–C and CC functional groups from Fourier transform infrared test results. The obtained CdSSe/GQDs composites demonstrated a superior photoelectric performance in comparison to the pure CdSSe semiconductor. The GQDs contents had a great effect on the photocurrent density and charge transfer resistance of the CdSSe/GQDs composites. As the amount of GQDs increased to 0.15 wt%, the photocurrent response reached 1.31 × 10−5 A/cm2, which was three times higher than that of CdSSe.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CdSSe
Graphene quantum dots
Photocurrent density

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CdSSe and CdSSe/GQDs composites

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of the GQDs (a), CdSSe (b) and CdSSe/GQDs composites (c).
AFM morphology (a), height distribution (b), TEM image (c) and HRTEM image (d) of GQDs, TEM image (e) and HRTEM image (f) of CdSSe/GQDs composites. EDX of CdSSe (g) and CdSSe/GQDs composites (h).
Survey (a) and high-resolution XPS spectra of Cd3d (b), S2p (c) and Se 3d (d) for CdSSe; survey (e) and high-resolution XPS spectra of Cd3d (f), S2p (g) and Se 3d (h) and C 1s (i) for CdSSe/GQDs.
Zeta potential of CdSSe, GQDs and CdSSe/GQDs composites.
PL spectra of GQDs (a) and CdSSe, CdSSe/GQDs composites (b).
FTIR spectra of CdSSe (a), CdSSe/GQDs-0.09 (b), CdSSe/GQDs-0.15 (c), CdSSe/GQDs-0.24 (d).
Transient photocurrent responses of CdSSe (a), CdSSe/GQDs-0.09 (b), CdSSe/GQDs-0.15 (c), CdSSe/GQDs-0.24 (d).
EIS spectra of CdSSe (a) and CdSSe/GQDs composites (b). Inset: equivalent electrical circuit.
Schematic illustration of photo-generated electrons transmission in CdSSe/GQDs composites.

head.ce:title: Effect of graphene quantum dots addition on photoelectric performances of CdSSe

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Resources and Environmental Engineering, Wuhan University of Technology, Wuhan 430070, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Resources and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Resources and Environmental Engineering, Wuhan University of Technology, Wuhan 430070, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Wuhan Qingjiang Chemical Co., LTD, Wuhan 430035, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Wuhan Qingjiang Chemical Co., LTD, Wuhan 430035, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: CdSSe/graphene quantum dots (CdSSe/GQDs) composites were prepared by a hydrothermal method in the way of electrostatic interaction. The structure, morphology and material performance were investigated by various physical and chemical analyses. According to the results of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, the GQDs were compounded into composites. Moreover, the composites contained C–O–C and CC functional groups from Fourier transform infrared test results. The obtained CdSSe/GQDs composites demonstrated a superior photoelectric performance in comparison to the pure CdSSe semiconductor. The GQDs contents had a great effect on the photocurrent density and charge transfer resistance of the CdSSe/GQDs composites. As the amount of GQDs increased to 0.15 wt%, the photocurrent response reached 1.31 × 10−5 A/cm2, which was three times higher than that of CdSSe.

ce:section.ce:para: Composites refered to a new type of materials which was composed of organic polymers, inorganic non-metals or metals and other types of materials through a composite process. It could not only retain the characteristics of the original materials, but also complement each other's properties. Kim et al. created carbon composite electrodes consisting of well-dispersed carbon nanotubes and percolated graphitic carbon domains [1]. Gerbaldi et al. reported the hybrid solid polymer electrolytes via solvent-free preparation process in ‘one pot’ for Li-based battery [2]. Scalia et al. proposed to make use of a multifunctional polymer electrolyte to integrate a dye-sensitized solar cell [3].

In recent years, ternary alloy semiconductors attracted widespread attention in various fields [4,5]. Therein, CdSSe, as one of the important II–VI ternary semiconductors with a band gap between 2.44 eV and 1.74 eV, had potential application in sensors, optoelectronic devices, solar battery, photovoltaic systems, etc. [6–9]. Since CdSSe was unstable and easy to aggregate ascribed to the high surface energy and small size [10], which were highly desirable for conductive materials to prevent the aggregation and promote the charge-transfer of CdSSe. Lu et al. synthesized ZnO/CdSSe core/shell nanowires with an improved photoresponsivity [11]. Chong et al. prepared the TiO2/CdSSe nanocomposites with an enhanced photocurrent [12]. Chen et al. reported a simple and low-cost approach to prepare CdS1-xSex/CdS nanocomposites [13]. In our previous work, CdS1-xSex/graphene composites were synthesized to present a higher photocurrent response as compared to pure CdS1-xSex, which was attributed to the introduction of micrometer-sized graphene sheets [14,15]. However, graphene sheets tended to aggregate in stacks, which greatly limited their photoelectric performances in nanometer-scale.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a new type of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, had the advantages of high specific surface area and excellent electrical conductivity. It was highly desirable for use as a zero-dimensional conductive carrier [16,17]. Zhou et al. employed a two-step reduction method to synthesize Au-GQDs @ Pt core-shell nanodendrites which exhibited an obviously enhancement in comparison to commercial Pt/C catalyst [18]. Wang et al. prepared GQDs coated Co3O4@CuO yolk-shell microspheres with an enhanced lithium storage behavior [19]. In our previous work of CdSe/GQDs [20], GQDs accelerated the photo-generated charge separation, shortened the conductive distance, and improved the electronic mobility of semiconductors. Though there were some researches about GQDs based composites, literatures on the incorporation of ternary CdSSe and GQDs were rarely discussed, and reports integrating interfacial charge-transfer and photocurrent responses were scarcely investigated.

Based on the above analysis and our previous work, a continuing effort was proposed to combine GQDs with ternary CdSSe, and investigate the effect of GQDs content on the photoelectric performances of CdSSe/GQDs composites. A series of CdSSe/GQDs composites with different GQDs contents were prepared by a one-step hydrothermal method and characterized by various analyses.

Graphite (99.9%), sulfuric acid (H2SO4), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), potassium permanganate (KMnO4), ethylene glycol (C2H6O2), nitric acid (HNO3), thiourea (H2NCSHN2), sodium selenite (Na2O3Se) and sodium nitrate (NaNO3) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), cadmium acetate (Cd(CH3COO)2), anhydrous ethanol (CH3CH2OH), hydrazine hydrate (N2H4·H2O) and polyethylene glycol (PEG) were obtained from Tianjin Krem Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd.

GO was prepared by an improved Hummers method using flake graphite as raw material to obtain GO after three stages of low temperature, medium temperature and high temperature [21]. First, graphene was prepared by heating graphite oxide at 400 °C for 0.5 h under N2 atmosphere [22]. Subsequently, the sulfuric acid, nitric acid and graphene were mixed and stirred for 10 h. The samples were collected by centrifugation and added in water. The pH of solution was adjusted to 8.0 using NaOH. The above solution was placed in the autoclave and kept at 200 °C for 10 h. Finally, GQDs were obtained through filtration dialysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CdSSe and CdSSe/GQDs composites
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis method of CdSSe/GQDs composites was a one-step hydrothermal method. Firstly, 2.66 g of cadmium acetate, 0.19 g of thiourea and 1.29 g of sodium selenite were dispersed in distilled water, respectively. Subsequently, the 10 mL of hydrazine hydrate and GQDs were added in the above-mixed solution and placed in the autoclave (180 °C, 8 h). Finally, the products were collected by centrifugal washing in water and ethanol and dried overnight at 60 °C. The composites were appointed to CdSSe/GQDs-X, where ‘X’ was the content of GQDs (0.09, 0.15 and 0.24 wt%).
CdSSe was synthesized by the same way of CdSSe/GQDs composites except for the absence of GQDs.

The crystal structure of samples was determined using an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, D/MAX-R8). The morphological characteristics were observed by a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100F) and an atomic force microscope (AFM, Multimode 8). The chemical components were attained by an X-ray photoelectron spectrum (XPS, ESCALAB 250XI). The surface charge of samples was determined using zeta potential (nano-ZS). FTIR spectra were obtained using a Fourier infrared spectrometer (Nicolet iS10). Photocurrent and impedance were tested using a three-electrode workstation (CHI650E).

XRD measurements were performed to study the crystalline structures of samples. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the GQDs displayed a broad peak centered at 26.28°. The crystallite size of the GQDs was calculated by the Scherrer equation. The Scherrer equation was D = Kλ/Bcosθ, Where K was the Scherrer constant, D was the average thickness of the grain perpendicular to the direction of the crystal plane, B was the half-width of the diffraction peak of the measured sample, θ was the diffraction angle, λ was the X-ray wavelength of 0.154056 nm. Finally, the calculation indicated that the crystallite size of the GQDs was 0.3385, which was consistent with TEM results [22]. As could be seen in Fig. 1b, there were six major diffraction peaks on the XRD pattern of CdSSe. The 2
θ
values were 24.12°, 25.6°, 27.18°, 42.222°, 46.4° and 49.86° corresponding to (100), (002), (101), (110), (103) and (200) crystals planes of hexagonal CdSSe, respectively [23]. It was found that the diffraction peaks were located between the positions of hexagonal phase CdS (JCPDS NO.800006) and hexagonal phase CdSe (JCPDS NO.020030). The diffraction peaks of CdSSe shifted toward lower 2θ compared to those of CdS. Such phenomenon was an indication of the increase in lattice parameter due to the large ionic radius of Se relative to that of S, which confirmed that the synthesized CdSSe was a hexagonal phase alloy. After adding GQDs, there was no significant shift in peak positions, which meant that the addition of GQDs did not change the lattice structure of CdSSe. Moreover, no diffraction peaks of GQDs were found in the XRD results of the CdSSe/GQDs composites [24,25]. The reason for this phenomenon might be that the amount of GQDs was too small, leading to an extremely low diffraction peak of GQDs.

The AFM morphology and height distribution of GQDs were shown in Fig. 2 a and b. The height of GQDs was approximately from 2.1 to 2.8 nm, and the average height was 2.4 nm corresponding to five graphene sheets. The TEM and HRTEM images of GQDs were shown in Fig. 2c and d. GQDs nanoparticles were evenly dispersed with the average size around 10 nm. The clear lattice spacing of 0.34 nm was assigned to the (002) crystal plane of GQDs [21]. This result was consistent with XRD result of GQDs. The TEM and HRTEM images of CdSSe/GQDs composites were shown in Fig. 2e and f. CdSSe/GQDs composites were rod-like particles with a size of 20 nm. The lattice fringes of 0.35 nm corresponded to the (002) interplanar spacing of CdSSe [26], and those of 0.21 nm and 0.34 nm corresponded to the (100) and (002) interplanar spacing of GQDs, respectively [27]. The EDX patterns of CdSSe and CdSSe/GQDs composites were recorded in Fig. 2g and h, in which the Cd, S, Se signals were originated from CdSSe, and the Cu peaks were ascribed to the copper mesh. By comparing the C element of CdSSe and CdSSe/GQDs, the C content of CdSSe/GQDs was obviously increased from 14.1% to 22.5% due to the addition of GQDs.

The chemical composition and surface elements of CdSSe and CdSSe/GQDs composites were characterized by XPS. A survey spectrum of CdSSe (Fig. 3 a) revealed the binding energies corresponding to Cd 3d, Se 3d, S 2p and 2s, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3b, the binding energy peaks located in 404.6 eV and 411.4 eV could be indexed to Cd 3d5/2 and Cd 3d3/2, respectively [28]. The deconvolution of the S 2p showed two peaks located at 160.5 eV and 165.1 eV in Fig. 3c [29]. The XPS spectrum of Se 3d (Fig. 3d) was divided into peaks at 53.6 eV and 54.2 eV, ascribed to Se 3d5/2 and Se 3d3/2 [30]. After GQDs were added into composites, the peaks of C 1s, O 1s, Cd 3d, Se 3d, S 2p and 2s could be observed from the XPS survey of CdSSe/GQDs composites (Fig. 3e). The peaks of Cd, S and Se in Fig. 3f–h were basically consistent with those in Fig. 3b–d mentioned above. The XPS spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 3i) was decomposed into three configurations. The typical peaks at 283.6 eV, 284.9 eV, and 286.5 eV were classed to the C–C, CC, and C–O bond, respectively [31,32]. Therefore, the XPS results confirmed that the GQDs were compounded into composites.

The surface charge was determined by Zeta potential as the result of CdSSe, GQDs and CdSSe/GQDs composites were demonstrated in Fig. 4 . The Zeta potential of CdSSe exhibited 9.22 mV, which demonstrated the positively charged character of the CdSSe surface. Moreover, the Zeta potential of GQDs was −10.41 mV ascribed to the abundant oxygen-containing functional groups on its surface, which was in favor of the GQDs to attach on the CdSSe surface via electrostatic attraction [33]. It led to a decrease of Zeta potential value from 9.22 mV to 2.16 mV following the anchor of GQDs to CdSSe surface. Therefore, the composites were formed through electrostatic attraction by CdSSe and GQDs.

Fig. 5 a showed the PL spectra of GQDs under the excitation wavelength from 420 nm to 460 nm. GQDs emitted a spectrum peaked at 502 nm as the excitation wavelength was 420 nm. Furthermore, the peak position presented a red-shift with the increase of excitation wavelength. From the results of zeta potential, the surface of GQDs contained oxygen-containing functional groups, which formed different energy levels resulting in a series of emission defect states [34]. Therefore, under different excitation wavelengths, GQDs would emit fluorescence that varies with the excitation wavelength. Fig. 5b showed the PL spectra of CdSSe and CdSSe/GQDs composites under excitation of 400 nm UV light. There was one characteristic peak of CdSSe located at 485 nm. The PL spectrum of CdSSe/GQDs composites displayed the same peak positions as CdSSe, while there was a decrease in PL intensity upon the addition of GQDs. This phenomenon was due to the declined recombination rate of the photoelectric hole pairs, resulting in the decrease in PL intensity [35].

The FTIR spectra of CdSSe, CdSSe/GQDs-0.09, CdSSe/GQDs-0.15 and CdSSe/GQDs-0.24 are shown in Fig. 6 . All samples had the obvious vibration peaks at 3446 cm−1 and 1633 cm−1, which could be deemed to the stretching vibrations of hydroxyl groups on account of adsorbed water molecule [36,13]. The vibrations at 1411 cm−1 were ascribed to the presence of CO2 from the air [13]. In addition, the CdSSe/GQDs composites had two stretching vibration peaks at 1563 cm−1 and 1004 cm−1 in comparison to CdSSe due to the GQDs. The peak at 1563 cm−1 could be corresponded to the stretching vibration of CC in GQDs [37]. The stretching vibration peak at 1004 cm−1 was caused by C–O–C [38]. This result also indicated that GQDs and CdSSe were compounded together. XPS analysis and Zeta potential also confirmed this result.

Transient photocurrent tests were recorded for typical on/off cycles of intermittent visible-light irradiation (60 s) light irradiation at a bias potential of 0.4 V. Fig. 7 showed the photocurrent-time responses of CdSSe and CdSSe/GQDs composites coated on FTO glasses. As shown in Fig. 7a, the photocurrent density of CdSSe was 0.43 × 10−5 A/cm2. After the introduction of GQDs, the photocurrent value of CdSSe/GQDs composites presented a significant improvement as can be seen from Fig. 7b–d. Furthermore, as the amount of GQDs increased to 0.15 wt%, the maximum photocurrent value of CdSSe/GQDs reached 1.31 × 10−5 A/cm2, which was three times as large as that of CdSSe. This phenomenon was ascribed to the existence of GQDs, which could promote the transfer of photoelectrons and reduce the electron-hole pairs recombination, resulting in the enhancement of the photocurrent responses of CdSSe/GQDs composites [39]. However, as the amount of GQDs further increased, the photocurrent density began to decrease due to the shadowing effect of excessive GQDs.

EIS measurements were investigated on the three-electrode systems and the spectra were collected after the signals were stable in the process of several cycles. Fig. 8 showed the EIS spectra and equivalent circuit (the inset) of CdSSe and CdSSe/GQDs composites. The diameter of the semicircle in the curve represented the charge transfer resistance (Rct) [40]. In addition, Rs, Rw and CPE were equivalent series resistance, Warburg impedance and double-layer capacitor, respectively [41]. CPE was a constant phase element representing the interfacial capacitance [42]. Warburg impedance was related to the diffusion of ion to the fabricated layer [43]. As could be seen from the figure, the diameter of the semicircle to CdSSe/GQDs composites was smaller than that of CdSSe. The Rct value of CdSSe/GQDs composites was 49.5 Ω, while the Rct value of CdSSe was 59.1 Ω. This result indicated that the GQDs could reduce the charge transfer resistance, leading to the improvement of photocurrent density.

Fig. 9 showed the electrons transfer process in CdSSe/GQDs composites based on the above results. When the light radiated on the CdSSe/GQDs composites, the electrons in CdSSe were excited from the VB to the CB. Subsequently, they were transferred to the FTO glass via GQDs, and finally conveyed to the external circuit. The introduction of GQDs improved the electron migration rate, caused to an enhancement in photocurrent responses, and decreased in charge transfer resistance.

In conclusion, we reported a simple one-step hydrothermal method to prepare CdSSe/GQDs composites. The results indicated that GQDs and CdSSe were combined through electrostatic attraction, and GQDs did not change the lattice structure of CdSSe. Concurrently, GQDs could reduce the electron-holes recombination and promote the transmission of photoelectron. The results led to a decrease in PL intensity and charge transfer resistance, and caused an enhancement in photocurrent responses. The photocurrent density as function of the GQDs contents with the optimum of 0.15 wt%. As the amount of GQDs was 0.15 wt%, the current density reached 1.31 × 10−5 A/cm2, which was three times higher than that of CdSSe.
This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [grant number 51204129].

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
